county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire. Early records sparse due to remote location and small population.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Wasco and Jackson counties in 1874. Named for the many lakes in the region. Lakeview has been the county seat since formation. Southeastern Oregon high desert county. Klamath County was carved from Lake County in 1882. Ranching and limited homesteading in an arid landscape. The Christmas Valley area saw a homesteading boom in the early 1900s that largely failed.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lake County is one of Oregon's most remote counties, and records are sparse for the early period. The county clerk in Lakeview has deed and marriage records from 1874. Klamath County was carved from Lake County in 1882, so pre-1882 records for the Klamath Basin area are in Lake County files. The Schminck Memorial Museum in Lakeview has ranching family files and local history. BLM GLO records document the failed homesteading ventures in Christmas Valley and Summer Lake — these entries include biographical data even for settlers who eventually abandoned their claims. The ZX Ranch and other large cattle operations' records are scattered. FamilySearch has limited microfilm for Lake County. The Warner Valley and Hart Mountain areas have connections to Northern Paiute peoples — BIA records at NARA Seattle cover these communities. Check Wasco and Jackson counties (parents) for pre-1874 records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from formation; in-office access only

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Includes ranching land and failed homestead entries in Christmas Valley.

source_profiles.source_name: BLM Homestead and Desert Land Patents — Lake County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for ranching homesteads and desert land entries. Many Christmas Valley and Summer Lake entries were eventually relinquished.
Marriage records from county formation. Very small volume.
Federal census 1880–1950 (1890 destroyed). Very small population throughout.

source_profiles.source_name: Lake County Examiner and Lakeview Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Lakeview newspapers covering ranching, homesteading, and remote southeastern Oregon community news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Limited digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Records well preserved from territorial era.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Linn and Benton counties in 1851, during the Oregon Territory period. Named for Joseph Lane, Oregon's first Territorial Governor. Eugene has been the county seat since 1853. Home to the University of Oregon (founded 1876). The Willamette Valley and Coast Range timber define the economy. Eugene was a major Donation Land Claim area.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lane County has excellent record survival from the territorial era. Donation Land Claims (1850–1855) for Lane County settlers are among the most genealogically valuable records — they contain biographical data, marriage information, and migration histories and are searchable at BLM GLO Records. The county clerk in Eugene has deed and marriage records from 1851. The Lane County Historical Museum has family files, photographs, and local history collections. The University of Oregon's Special Collections holds Willamette Valley manuscript collections, logging industry records, and Oregon Country history materials. FamilySearch has extensive microfilm of Lane County records including marriage, probate, and land records. The Eugene Pioneer Cemetery has burials from the 1850s. The Coast Range logging operations generated company town records and employment files — check the Oregon Historical Society for lumber company papers. Florence on the coast has separate fishing and shipping records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from territorial era; online index available for recent records

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from territorial era. Includes Donation Land Claim transfers and Willamette Valley agricultural land.
Donation Land Claim files for Lane County settlers with detailed biographical information.
Marriage records from territorial period. FamilySearch has microfilmed early Lane County records.
Federal census 1860–1950 (1890 destroyed). Includes agricultural schedules for Willamette Valley farmers.

source_profiles.repository: Lane County Circuit Court / Oregon State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate case files, wills, and estate inventories from territorial era. Donation Land Claim settlers' estates are detailed.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — older records at Oregon State Archives.

source_profiles.source_name: Eugene Guard and Lane County Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Eugene and Lane County newspapers covering university town, timber industry, and Willamette Valley agricultural news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library and University of Oregon.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Records from formation are intact.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Benton and Polk counties in 1893. Named for Abraham Lincoln. Newport has been the county seat since formation. Central Oregon coast county with fishing, tourism, and timber industries. The Siletz Reservation occupies the northern portion — the Confederated Tribes of Siletz Indians were terminated in 1954 and restored in 1977. Newport and Toledo are the main towns.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lincoln County has two significant genealogical tracks: the coastal fishing and timber communities and the Confederated Tribes of Siletz Indians. The Siletz Reservation was created in 1855 as a relocation site for dozens of western Oregon tribes displaced by the Rogue River Wars and other conflicts. BIA records at NARA Seattle for the Siletz Agency are extensive and cover Siletz, Rogue River, Tolowa, Chetco, and other peoples forcibly relocated there. The tribe was terminated in 1954 and restored in 1977 — termination rolls list all members. The county clerk in Newport has deed and marriage records from 1893. For pre-1893 records, check Benton and Polk counties (parents). The Lincoln County Historical Society has fishing industry records and family files. The Bayshore/Nye Beach area of Newport was a resort destination from the 1890s. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Yaquina Bay oyster industry records document immigrant workers.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from formation; limited online access

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Includes coastal resort properties and fishing industry waterfront land.

source_profiles.source_name: Confederated Tribes of Siletz Indians — BIA and Termination Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: BIA records for the Siletz Reservation — relocation site for dozens of western Oregon tribal groups. Census rolls, allotment records, agency correspondence, and termination rolls (1954). Tribe restored 1977.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent — NARA Seattle holds comprehensive Siletz Agency records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation.
Federal census from 1900 (first after county formation). Pre-1893 area under Benton and Polk counties.

source_profiles.source_name: Newport News and Lincoln County Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Newport and central Oregon coast newspapers covering fishing, tourism, and Siletz Reservation news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Records well preserved from Provisional Government era.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of Oregon's earliest counties, formed during the Provisional Government period in 1847 from Champoeg County. Named for Lewis Fields Linn, U.S. Senator from Missouri who promoted the Oregon Donation Land Claim Act. Albany has been the county seat since 1851. Willamette Valley agricultural county with extensive Donation Land Claims. The Santiam Wagon Road connected the valley to eastern Oregon.
county_metadata.research_tips: Linn County is one of Oregon's Provisional Government era counties with records reaching back to the late 1840s. Donation Land Claims for Linn County settlers (1850–1855) are key genealogical resources at BLM GLO Records. The county clerk in Albany has deed and marriage records from the 1840s. The Albany Regional Museum has family files, photograph collections, and local history materials. The Linn County Historical Museum in Brownsville has pioneer family files and Brownsville (one of Oregon's oldest towns) records. FamilySearch has extensive microfilm of Linn County records from the territorial era including probate and marriage files. The Santiam Wagon Road (1860s) brought traffic through Linn County to eastern Oregon — toll road records may be at the Oregon Historical Society. Lebanon and Sweet Home were later timber industry centers with separate community records. Check Champoeg County records at Oregon State Archives for the earliest entries.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from Provisional Government era; online index available

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from Provisional Government era. Includes Donation Land Claim transfers and Willamette Valley agricultural land.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — excellent preservation from the territorial period.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Donation Land Claim files for Linn County settlers with detailed biographical data including marriage dates and emigration details.
Marriage records from Provisional Government era. Among Oregon's earliest civil marriage records.
Federal census 1850–1950 (1890 destroyed). 1850 territorial census includes Linn County. Agricultural schedules available.

source_profiles.repository: Linn County Circuit Court / Oregon State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate files, wills, and estate inventories from Provisional Government era. Pioneer settlers' estates are particularly detailed.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — older records at Oregon State Archives.

source_profiles.source_name: Albany Democrat-Herald and Linn County Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Albany and Linn County newspapers covering agriculture, Santiam Wagon Road, and Willamette Valley community news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Records preserved from formation.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Baker County in 1887. Named for the Malheur River (from French 'malheur' meaning misfortune). Vale has been the county seat since formation. Oregon's second-largest county by area, bordering Idaho and Nevada. Japanese farming communities developed around Ontario and Nyssa in the early 1900s. Significant Basque sheepherding community. The Oregon Trail followed the Snake River through the county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Malheur County's genealogical landscape includes Oregon Trail emigrants, ranchers, Japanese farmers, and Basque sheepherders. The county clerk in Vale has deed and marriage records from 1887. Japanese families settled around Ontario and Nyssa from the early 1900s, developing sugar beet farming and onion growing — during WWII some Japanese Americans were sent to Malheur County as an alternative to internment camps (the only county in America to accept Japanese Americans). Japanese community records are at the Four Rivers Cultural Center in Ontario. The Basque sheepherding community is documented in oral histories at the Oregon Historical Society and UO Libraries. BLM GLO homestead patents cover the ranching and farming land entries. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Check Baker County (parent) for pre-1887 records. The Snake River/Oregon Trail emigrant crossing sites are documented in diaries at the Oregon Historical Society.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from formation; limited online access

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Includes ranching, irrigation farming, and Japanese farmer land purchases.

source_profiles.repository: Four Rivers Cultural Center, Ontario / Oregon Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records of Japanese farming families in the Ontario and Nyssa area. Malheur County uniquely accepted Japanese Americans during WWII as an alternative to internment camps.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — Four Rivers Cultural Center has compiled community histories and oral histories.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation. Includes Japanese and Basque immigrant families.
Federal census 1890–1950 (1890 destroyed). Shows diverse farming communities including Japanese and Basque families.
Federal land patents for ranching homesteads and irrigation farming land in eastern Oregon.

source_profiles.source_name: Ontario Argus Observer and Malheur County Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Ontario and Vale newspapers covering ranching, Japanese farming communities, and Oregon Trail commemoration.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire. Some very early Provisional Government records are fragmentary.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of the four original districts created by the Provisional Government in 1843. Named for Francis Marion, the 'Swamp Fox' of the American Revolution. Salem has been the county seat since 1851 and has served as Oregon's capital since 1852. Houses the Oregon State Capitol, Oregon State Archives, and Oregon State Library. The Chemeketa (Kalapuya) people originally inhabited the area. Major Willamette Valley agricultural county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Marion County is Oregon's capital county and houses the Oregon State Archives in Salem, making it a dual research location — county records AND state records are in the same city. The Oregon State Archives holds Provisional Government records (1843–1848), territorial records (1848–1859), and state agency records. The county clerk has deed and marriage records from the 1840s. Donation Land Claims for Marion County settlers are extensive at BLM GLO Records and represent some of Oregon's earliest settlers. The Oregon State Hospital (1883) records, now at the Oregon State Archives, document patients from across the state. Willamette University (1842, Oregon's oldest university) records are available through the university archives. The Marion County Historical Society has family files. FamilySearch has extensive Marion County microfilm. The Chemawa Indian School (1880–present) in Salem — the oldest continuously operating Indian boarding school in the US — has records at NARA and the school itself.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from Provisional Government era; online index available

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from Provisional Government era — among Oregon's oldest land records. Includes Donation Land Claim transfers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — excellent preservation from the earliest period.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Donation Land Claim files for Marion County settlers — among the most numerous in Oregon.
Student records from Chemawa Indian School (the oldest continuously operating Indian boarding school in the US). Documents Native American students from across the Pacific Northwest.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — NARA Seattle holds historical records. Current records at the school.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from Provisional Government era — among Oregon's earliest civil marriage records.
Federal census 1850–1950 (1890 destroyed). 1850 territorial census includes Marion County — Oregon's capital.
Oregon Statesman (founded 1851) and other Salem newspapers covering state government, agriculture, and community news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; Oregon State Library has extensive microfilm holdings.

county_metadata.record_losses: The Heppner Flood of 1903 was devastating to the town (killing 247 people) but courthouse records on the hill survived the floodwaters.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Umatilla County in 1885. Named for J.L. Morrow, a prominent settler. Heppner has been the county seat since formation. The Heppner Flood of June 14, 1903 — a flash flood that killed approximately 247 people — was one of Oregon's worst natural disasters. Wheat farming and cattle ranching county in the Columbia Plateau.
county_metadata.research_tips: Morrow County research is profoundly affected by the Heppner Flood of 1903, which killed approximately 247 people (nearly a third of the town's population) and destroyed many private records, business records, and family documents — though the courthouse on higher ground survived with its official records intact. The county clerk has deed and marriage records from 1885. Researchers tracing Heppner families should cross-reference the flood victim lists compiled by the Morrow County Museum and historical society. The Morrow County Heritage Museum in Heppner has flood documentation, family files, and photographs. FamilySearch has some microfilmed Morrow County records. BLM GLO homestead patents cover wheat farming homesteads. Check Umatilla County (parent) for pre-1885 records. The Boardman Bombing Range (WWII) and later Umatilla Army Depot generated military records at NARA. Irrigon and Boardman are newer communities developed with Columbia Basin irrigation.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from formation; in-office access only

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Wheat farming homesteads and ranching land.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — courthouse records survived the 1903 Heppner Flood.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation. Note 1903 flood impact on Heppner families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — courthouse records survived the flood.

source_profiles.source_name: Heppner Flood Victim Records and Death Certificates
source_profiles.repository: Morrow County Heritage Museum / Oregon State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Death records and victim lists from the June 14, 1903 Heppner Flood that killed approximately 247 people. Compiled lists include victims' names, ages, and family relationships.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — multiple sources have compiled victim lists. Museum has detailed documentation.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1890–1950 (1890 destroyed). 1900 census is valuable as it documents pre-flood Heppner population.

source_profiles.source_name: Heppner Gazette-Times and Morrow County Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Heppner newspapers including flood coverage. The Heppner Gazette published flood aftermath accounts.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; newspaper office records also affected by the flood.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire. Some early Portland city records were lost in fires, but county records are intact.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Washington and Clackamas counties in 1854. Named for the Multnomah people (Chinookan). Portland, Oregon's largest city, has been the county seat since formation. Portland was founded in 1845 at the confluence of the Willamette and Columbia rivers and became the Pacific Northwest's commercial hub. The smallest county in Oregon by area but the most populous. Major port city with diverse immigrant communities including Chinese, Japanese, Scandinavian, and Eastern European populations.
county_metadata.research_tips: Multnomah County is Oregon's most populous county and Portland is the state's primary urban center, creating the richest and most diverse genealogical record set. The county clerk has deed and marriage records from 1854. The Oregon Historical Society (OHS) in Portland is the premier repository for Oregon genealogy — its research library holds manuscript collections, family files, photograph archives, and the Oregon Historical Quarterly. Portland's Chinatown records, Chinese Exclusion Act case files at NARA Seattle, and the Oregon Chinese Consolidated Benevolent Association records document the Chinese community. Japanese American records include pre-war community organizations and WWII internment documentation at the Oregon Nikkei Endowment. The Multnomah County Library's genealogy collection is extensive. Portland city directories from the 1860s are at OHS. FamilySearch has extensive county microfilm. The Port of Portland records document shipping and immigration. Check the Archdiocese of Portland Catholic archives for church records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County recording office with deed records from formation; online index and images available

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Portland urban property records are among the most detailed in Oregon.

source_profiles.source_name: Portland Chinese Exclusion Act Case Files and Chinatown Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Chinese Exclusion Act case files for Portland — one of the largest Chinese communities on the West Coast. Includes photographs, affidavits, merchant certificates, and laborer records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent — NARA Seattle holds extensive Portland district case files.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation. High volume reflecting Portland's urban population.
Federal census 1860–1950 (1890 destroyed). Portland's diverse population makes census records particularly valuable. Includes Chinese, Japanese, and European immigrant communities.

source_profiles.repository: Oregon Historical Society / Multnomah County Library
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Annual Portland city directories listing residents, occupations, and addresses. Essential for tracking urban families between census years.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — nearly complete run at OHS from 1863.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Naturalization records from Portland federal and county courts. Documents diverse immigrant communities including Chinese, Japanese, Scandinavian, and Eastern European applicants.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — NARA Seattle holds federal court naturalization records.

source_profiles.repository: Chronicling America / Oregon Historical Society / Multnomah County Library
source_profiles.coverage_notes: The Oregonian (founded 1850) — Oregon's newspaper of record — and other Portland papers. Obituaries, legal notices, shipping news, and community events.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good digitization for The Oregonian. Other Portland papers partially digitized.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Records from Provisional Government era survive well.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of Oregon's earliest counties, formed in 1845 during the Provisional Government period. Named for President James K. Polk. Dallas has been the county seat since 1856 (earlier seat at Cynthia Ann). Willamette Valley agricultural county. The Confederated Tribes of Grand Ronde Reservation is partly in western Polk County — terminated in 1954, restored in 1983. Fort Yamhill (1856–1866) guarded the Grand Ronde Reservation.
county_metadata.research_tips: Polk County is one of Oregon's earliest Provisional Government counties with records from the mid-1840s. The county clerk in Dallas has deed and marriage records from the 1840s. Donation Land Claims for Polk County are extensive at BLM GLO Records. The Confederated Tribes of Grand Ronde Reservation straddles the Polk/Yamhill county line — BIA records at NARA Seattle for the Grand Ronde Agency are essential for Native American research, covering dozens of tribal groups relocated there in the 1850s. Fort Yamhill (1856–1866) military records at NARA document soldiers who guarded the reservation. The Polk County Historical Society in Monmouth has family files and photographs. FamilySearch has microfilmed Polk County records from the territorial era. Western Oregon University in Monmouth (founded 1856 as a teacher training school) records are in its archives. Check Yamhill District records at Oregon State Archives for the earliest entries.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from Provisional Government era; limited online access

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from Provisional Government era. Includes Donation Land Claim transfers and Willamette Valley agricultural land.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — excellent preservation from earliest period.

source_profiles.source_name: Confederated Tribes of Grand Ronde — BIA Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA Seattle / Grand Ronde Tribal Office
source_profiles.coverage_notes: BIA records for the Grand Ronde Reservation — relocation site for many western Oregon tribal groups. Census rolls, allotment records, and termination files (1954). Tribe restored 1983.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — NARA Seattle holds Grand Ronde Agency records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Donation Land Claim files for Polk County settlers — extensive claims in the Willamette Valley.
Marriage records from Provisional Government era.
Federal census 1850–1950 (1890 destroyed). 1850 territorial census includes Polk County.
Military records from Fort Yamhill, which guarded the Grand Ronde Reservation. Philip Sheridan served here as a young officer. Personnel and post records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — NARA holds federal military records.

source_profiles.source_name: Dallas Itemizer-Observer and Polk County Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Dallas and Polk County newspapers covering agriculture, Grand Ronde Reservation news, and Willamette Valley community events.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library.
